title: Dog Intermittent Skipping on Back Leg: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog skipping on a back leg? Learn about luxating patella, urgency levels, and why capturing a video is vital for your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is Intermittent Skipping?

Intermittent skipping, often described as a dog 'hitching' their leg or 'bunny hopping' for a few steps before returning to a normal gait, is a common orthopedic observation. In many cases, this is a classic sign of a luxating patella, where the kneecap temporarily slides out of its groove. Other potential causes include early-stage cranial cruciate ligament (CCL) disease or minor soft tissue strains.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for intermittent skipping is typically Low. If your dog is still eating, drinking, and able to walk comfortably most of the time, this is not a life-threatening emergency. however, it does warrant a scheduled veterinary exam within the next week. If the skipping becomes a permanent limp, or if your dog is in visible distress or refusing to put any weight on the leg, the urgency increases to moderate.

Why Video and Photos Help Triage

Because this behavior is intermittent, your dog will often walk perfectly normal the moment they enter a veterinary clinic due to adrenaline. Capturing a video of the skipping motion at home is incredibly helpful for triage. It allows the veterinarian to see the frequency, the specific leg involved, and the severity of the 'skip,' which leads to a faster and more accurate diagnosis during your appointment.

faqs.question: What is a luxating patella?
faqs.answer: A luxating patella occurs when the dog's kneecap shifts out of its normal alignment, often causing the dog to skip or hold the leg up until the bone pops back into place.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medication?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human medications like ibuprofen or acetaminophen, as these can be toxic. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery for skipping?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Treatment depends on the 'grade' of the condition. Mild cases may be managed with weight control and joint supplements, while more severe cases may require surgical correction.
